1:
"My brethren, have not the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with respect of persons." The witnesses note the warning against holding the faith with partiality. On "have not the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ... with respect of persons," the grammatical voices note the command. Barnes and Robertson note the prohibition: "have not (do not hold) the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ... with respect of persons (prosopolempsiais — partiality, favouritism, judging by outward appearance)" — the profession of faith in Christ must not be joined with partiality toward persons; the believer is not to favour the rich over the poor.

On "the Lord of glory," the witnesses note Christ's dignity. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ is called "the Lord of glory (the glorious Lord, or the Lord who is the glory)" — a title of his divine majesty; before so glorious a Lord, earthly distinctions of wealth and rank are nothing, making partiality unfitting.

On "respect of persons," the eastern Cyril notes the inconsistency: partiality based on outward rank contradicts the faith of Christ, who came for all and esteemed not the outward; to honour the rich and slight the poor in the assembly betrays the gospel's spirit. The verse displays the warning against holding the faith with partiality.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prohibition — "have not (hold not) the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ... with respect of persons (partiality)," the profession of faith not to be joined with favouritism (Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord of glory," Christ's divine majesty making earthly distinctions nothing (Barnes, Calvin); partiality contradicting the gospel's spirit, which esteems not the outward (Cyril). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the faith: of Christ. The verse displays the Lord: of glory. The verse displays the prohibition: respect of persons. The verse displays the inconsistency: with the gospel. The command displays faith in Christ excluding partiality. The verse displays the brethren: addressed. The verse displays the partiality: forbidden. The verse displays the glory: Christ's. The warning against holding the faith with partiality displays the opening of the chapter: the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, must not be held with respect of persons — favouritism toward the rich and contempt for the poor is incompatible with the gospel. The verse displays the impartial faith, illustrated by the rich and poor in the assembly (vv.2-3).
2:
"For if there come unto your assembly a man with a gold ring, in goodly apparel, and there come in also a poor man in vile raiment;" The brief verse notes the illustration of the rich and poor entering the assembly. The grammatical voices note the scene. Barnes and Robertson note the supposed case: "if there come unto your assembly (synagogen — gathering, meeting) a man with a gold ring (gold-ringed, a mark of wealth), in goodly apparel (splendid clothing), and there come in also a poor man in vile (shabby, dirty) raiment" — James pictures two men entering the Christian assembly, one richly dressed, one poor and shabby.

On "a man with a gold ring, in goodly apparel," the witnesses note the rich man. Barnes and Clarke note the marks of wealth: the gold ring and splendid clothing signal a rich, distinguished visitor; the outward tokens of rank and means.

On "a poor man in vile raiment," the grammatical-historical note the contrast: the poor man "in vile raiment (mean, soiled clothing)" stands in sharp contrast — outwardly unimpressive, lowly; the two are set side by side to test how the assembly will treat them. The verse displays the illustration of the rich and poor entering the assembly.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the supposed case — "if there come unto your assembly (gathering) a man with a gold ring, in goodly apparel, and... a poor man in vile (shabby) raiment," two men entering, one richly dressed, one poor (Barnes, Robertson); the gold ring and splendid clothing marking the rich visitor (Barnes, Clarke); the poor man in mean clothing the sharp contrast (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the two visitors. The verse marks the assembly: entered. The verse displays the rich: gold ring, fine clothes. The verse displays the poor: vile raiment. The verse displays the contrast: rank and lowliness. The scene displays the two men set side by side. The verse displays the gold ring: worn. The verse displays the apparel: goodly. The verse displays the raiment: vile. The illustration of the rich and poor entering the assembly displays the test of partiality: when a richly dressed man with a gold ring and a poor man in shabby clothing both enter the gathering, the assembly's treatment of them will reveal whether it judges by outward appearance. The verse displays the two visitors, whose unequal welcome is now described (v.3).
3:
"And ye have respect to him that weareth the gay clothing, and say unto him, Sit thou here in a good place; and say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool:" The witnesses note the partial welcome given to rich and poor. On "ye have respect to him that weareth the gay clothing," the grammatical voices note the favouritism. Barnes and Robertson note the partial treatment: "ye have respect to (look upon with favour, give special attention to) him that weareth the gay (splendid, fine) clothing" — the rich visitor is shown deference and honour because of his fine clothes.

On "Sit thou here in a good place," the witnesses note the honour to the rich. Barnes and JFB note that the rich man is invited to "sit here in a good place (an honourable seat)" — given the place of honour, the best seat, on account of his wealth and dress.

On "say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool," the grammatical-historical note the slight to the poor: the poor man is told to "stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool (on the floor, at one's feet)" — assigned a place of dishonour, made to stand or to sit on the ground; the contrast between the honour given the rich and the contempt shown the poor exposes the partiality. The verse displays the partial welcome given to rich and poor.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the favouritism — "ye have respect to (favour) him that weareth the gay (fine) clothing," the rich shown deference for his dress (Barnes, Robertson); "Sit thou here in a good place," the rich given the honourable seat (Barnes, JFB); "say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit under my footstool," the poor assigned dishonour, made to stand or sit on the ground (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the unequal welcome. The verse marks the respect: to the rich. The verse displays the honour: a good place. The verse displays the slight: to the poor. The verse displays the dishonour: stand, or under the footstool. The welcome displays the rich honoured and the poor despised. The verse displays the gay clothing: favoured. The verse displays the good place: given. The verse displays the poor: slighted. The partial welcome given to rich and poor displays the sin of favouritism in action: the richly dressed man is shown deference and given the place of honour, while the poor man is told to stand or sit on the floor — the assembly judging by outward appearance. The verse displays the partiality, which makes them evil-thinking judges (v.4).
4:
"Are ye not then partial in yourselves, and are become judges of evil thoughts?" The brief verse notes the charge of being partial judges with evil thoughts. The grammatical voices note the conviction. Barnes and Robertson note the accusing question: "are ye not then partial in yourselves (made distinctions among yourselves, wavered, divided), and are become judges of evil thoughts (judges with evil reasonings, or judges who reason wickedly)" — by such favouritism they have made unjust distinctions and become judges governed by evil motives.

On "partial in yourselves," the witnesses note the inward division. Barnes and Calvin note that "partial in yourselves" means they made a distinction, wavered, or were divided in their own minds — judging by the wrong standard (wealth) rather than by faith and worth.

On "judges of evil thoughts," the grammatical-historical note the corrupt judgment: they are "judges of evil thoughts (judges with wicked reasonings)" — in honouring the rich and slighting the poor, they judge from corrupt motives, becoming judges whose reasoning is evil; partiality corrupts judgment. The verse displays the charge of being partial judges with evil thoughts.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conviction — "are ye not then partial in yourselves (made unjust distinctions), and are become judges of evil thoughts (with evil reasonings)," making unjust distinctions from evil motives (Barnes, Robertson); "partial in yourselves" the inward division, judging by wealth not worth (Barnes, Calvin); "judges of evil thoughts," corrupt judgment from wicked reasoning (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the partiality: in yourselves. The verse displays the judges: of evil thoughts. The verse displays the distinction: unjust. The verse displays the motive: corrupt. The charge displays partiality making them evil-reasoning judges. The verse displays the partiality: convicted. The verse displays the judging: evil. The verse displays the thoughts: wicked. The charge of being partial judges with evil thoughts displays the conviction of the favouritism: by honouring the rich and slighting the poor, they have made unjust distinctions and become judges governed by evil reasonings — partiality has corrupted their judgment. The verse displays the evil-reasoning judges, rebuked by God's choice of the poor (v.5).
5:
"Hearken, my beloved brethren, Hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom which he hath promised to them that love him?" The witnesses note that God has chosen the poor to be rich in faith and heirs. On "hath not God chosen the poor of this world," the grammatical voices note the divine choice. Barnes and Robertson note the rebuke of partiality by God's own pattern: "hath not God chosen the poor of this world (the poor in worldly goods)" — God himself has chosen many of the poor; the gospel has been received chiefly by the lowly, so to despise the poor is to despise those God has chosen.

On "rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom," the witnesses note their spiritual wealth. Barnes and Calvin note that the poor God chooses are "rich in faith (made spiritually rich though poor in goods), and heirs of the kingdom" — though destitute in this world, they are rich in faith and heirs of God's kingdom; their true wealth is spiritual.

On "which he hath promised to them that love him," the puritan Henry notes the promise: the kingdom is "promised to them that love him" — to those who love God; the poor believers, loving God, inherit the kingdom; love for God, not worldly wealth, marks the heirs. The verse displays that God has chosen the poor to be rich in faith and heirs.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine choice — "hath not God chosen the poor of this world," God choosing many of the poor, the gospel received chiefly by the lowly (Barnes, Robertson); "rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom," the poor made spiritually rich and heirs, their wealth spiritual (Barnes, Calvin); "promised to them that love him," the kingdom inherited by those who love God (Henry). The verse displays God's choice. The verse marks the choosing: the poor. The verse displays the riches: in faith. The verse displays the inheritance: the kingdom. The verse displays the recipients: those who love him. The choice displays God honouring the poor whom men despise. The verse displays the poor: chosen. The verse displays the faith: rich. The verse displays the kingdom: inherited. That God has chosen the poor to be rich in faith and heirs displays the rebuke of partiality: God himself has chosen many of the poor of this world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom promised to those who love him — so to despise the poor is to dishonour those God has honoured. The verse displays God's chosen poor, against whom the readers had shown contempt (v.6).
6:
"But ye have despised the poor. Do not rich men oppress you, and draw you before the judgment seats?" The witnesses note the rebuke: despising the poor while the rich oppress. On "ye have despised the poor," the grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note the direct rebuke: "ye have despised (dishonoured, treated with contempt) the poor" — in their partiality they had dishonoured the very people God had honoured; despising the poor contradicts God's choice (v.5).

On "do not rich men oppress you," the witnesses note the irony. Barnes and Calvin note the pointed question: "do not rich men oppress (exploit, tyrannize over) you" — the very rich they flattered were often the ones who oppressed them; it was foolish as well as wrong to honour those who abused them.

On "draw you before the judgment seats," the grammatical-historical note the persecution: the rich "draw you before the judgment seats (drag you to court, to the tribunals)" — they hauled the believers before courts, persecuting and prosecuting them; the very class being favoured was the persecuting class. The verse displays the rebuke: despising the poor while the rich oppress.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "ye have despised (dishonoured) the poor," dishonouring those God honoured, contradicting his choice (Barnes, Robertson); "do not rich men oppress (exploit) you," the flattered rich often the oppressors, making the partiality foolish (Barnes, Calvin); "draw you before the judgment seats," the rich dragging believers to court, the persecuting class (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rebuke. The verse marks the despising: of the poor. The verse displays the irony: the rich oppress. The verse displays the persecution: drawn to court. The verse displays the folly: honouring oppressors. The rebuke displays the poor despised, the rich oppressors flattered. The verse displays the poor: dishonoured. The verse displays the rich: oppressing. The verse displays the judgment seats: dragged to. The rebuke — despising the poor while the rich oppress — displays the folly and wrong of the partiality: they had dishonoured the poor whom God chose, while flattering the rich, who were often the very ones oppressing them and dragging them before the courts. The verse displays the oppressing rich, who also blaspheme Christ's name (v.7).
7:
"Do not they blaspheme that worthy name by the which ye are called?" The brief verse notes that the rich oppressors blaspheme Christ's worthy name. The grammatical voices note the further charge. Barnes and Robertson note the climax of the indictment of the rich: "do not they blaspheme (revile, speak evil of) that worthy (honourable, excellent) name by the which ye are called (the name of Christ, named upon you)" — the rich oppressors not only persecuted the believers but blasphemed the very name of Christ by which they were called.

On "that worthy name," the witnesses note the name of Christ. Barnes and Calvin note that "that worthy name" is the name of Christ (or of God), the honourable name borne by Christians; "by which ye are called" — named upon them at their profession (perhaps at baptism); the name that marks them as Christ's.

On "blaspheme," the grammatical-historical note the aggravation: the rich "blaspheme" that name — revile and dishonour Christ himself; so to flatter such blasphemers, while despising the poor who honour Christ, was doubly perverse. The verse displays that the rich oppressors blaspheme Christ's worthy name.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the further charge — "do not they blaspheme (revile) that worthy (honourable) name by the which ye are called," the rich oppressors blaspheming the name of Christ named upon the believers (Barnes, Robertson); "that worthy name" the name of Christ borne by Christians, named upon them at their profession (Barnes, Calvin); the blasphemy aggravating the folly of flattering such men (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blasphemed name. The verse marks the blasphemy: of the name. The verse displays the name: worthy, Christ's. The verse displays the calling: named upon them. The verse displays the perversity: flattering blasphemers. The charge displays the rich reviling the name the believers bear. The verse displays the blaspheming: by the rich. The verse displays the name: honourable. The verse displays the called: by it. That the rich oppressors blaspheme Christ's worthy name displays the climax of the indictment: the very rich being flattered not only oppressed the believers but blasphemed the honourable name of Christ by which they were called — making the partiality doubly perverse. The verse displays the blasphemers, against the royal law of love (v.8).
8:
"If ye fulfil the royal law according to the scripture, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself, ye do well:" The witnesses note the royal law of love to the neighbour. On "if ye fulfil the royal law according to the scripture," the grammatical voices note the supreme law. Barnes and Robertson note the standard: "if ye fulfil the royal law (basilikon — kingly, sovereign, supreme) according to the scripture (as written, Leviticus 19:18)" — the law of love is called "royal" as the chief, the king of laws, supreme over all duties to others.

On "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself," the witnesses note the command. Barnes and Calvin note the content: "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself" — the great commandment summing up duty to others; impartial love to all, rich and poor alike, fulfils this royal law; partiality violates it.

On "ye do well," the puritan Henry notes the commendation: if they truly keep this royal law of impartial love, "ye do well" — they act rightly; the test of their conduct toward rich and poor is whether it springs from genuine love to the neighbour, which knows no partiality. The verse displays the royal law of love to the neighbour.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the supreme law — "if ye fulfil the royal (kingly, sovereign) law according to the scripture (Leviticus 19:18)," the law of love the chief, the king of laws (Barnes, Robertson); "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself," the great commandment, impartial love fulfilling it (Barnes, Calvin); "ye do well," acting rightly if love is genuine and impartial (Henry). The verse displays the royal law. The verse marks the law: royal. The verse displays the source: the scripture. The verse displays the command: love thy neighbour. The verse displays the commendation: ye do well. The law displays impartial love as the supreme rule. The verse displays the royal: law. The verse displays the love: of neighbour. The verse displays the doing: well. The royal law of love to the neighbour displays the standard by which partiality is judged: the kingly law of Scripture, "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself," requires impartial love to all — and if they truly keep it, they do well. The verse displays the royal law, violated by respect of persons (v.9).
9:
"But if ye have respect to persons, ye commit sin, and are convinced of the law as transgressors." The brief verse notes that partiality is sin, convicting them as transgressors. The grammatical voices note the verdict. Barnes and Robertson note the conclusion: "if ye have respect to persons (show partiality), ye commit sin (it is sin), and are convinced (convicted, exposed) of the law as transgressors" — partiality is a positive sin, and the law itself convicts the partial as lawbreakers.

On "ye commit sin," the witnesses note the guilt. Barnes and Calvin note that respect of persons is not a small fault but actual sin — a breach of the royal law of love; it cannot be excused as a trifle.

On "convinced of the law as transgressors," the grammatical-historical note the conviction: they are "convinced (convicted) of (by) the law as transgressors" — the law that commands impartial love pronounces them guilty; in showing partiality they break the law and stand condemned by it as transgressors. The verse displays that partiality is sin, convicting them as transgressors.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the verdict — "if ye have respect to persons (show partiality), ye commit sin (it is sin), and are convinced (convicted) of the law as transgressors," partiality a positive sin convicting them as lawbreakers (Barnes, Robertson); "ye commit sin," not a small fault but actual sin, a breach of the royal law (Barnes, Calvin); "convinced of the law as transgressors," the law pronouncing them guilty (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the conviction. The verse marks the partiality: sin. The verse displays the guilt: committed. The verse displays the conviction: by the law. The verse displays the verdict: transgressors. The verdict displays partiality convicting them as lawbreakers. The verse displays the respect: of persons. The verse displays the sin: committed. The verse displays the transgressors: convicted. That partiality is sin, convicting them as transgressors, displays the verdict on respect of persons: to show partiality is to commit sin and to be convicted by the law itself as a transgressor — for it breaks the royal law of love. The verse displays the convicting law, which is broken whole by one offence (v.10).
10:
"For whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all." The witnesses note the principle that one offence makes one guilty of all. On "whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point," the grammatical voices note the unity of the law. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend (stumble, fail) in one point (a single command), he is guilty of all" — the law is a unity; to break one part is to become a transgressor of the law as a whole, guilty before it.

On "guilty of all," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Calvin note the careful sense: "guilty of all" does not mean that one who breaks one command is as guilty as if he had broken every command, but that he has become a transgressor of the law itself — he has broken the law (which is one), and so stands guilty before it; one breach makes him a lawbreaker.

On the unity of the law, the grammatical-historical note the ground: the law is the expression of one will of one Lawgiver (v.11); to violate any part is to set oneself against that will and to break the whole; hence partiality, though "one point," makes one a transgressor of the law. The verse displays the principle that one offence makes one guilty of all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all," the law a unity, one breach making one a transgressor of the whole (Barnes, Robertson); "guilty of all" meaning not equally guilty as if breaking every command, but having become a lawbreaker, guilty before the law (Barnes, Calvin); the law one will of one Lawgiver, any breach setting one against it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the law's unity. The verse marks the keeping: the whole law. The verse displays the offence: one point. The verse displays the guilt: of all. The verse displays the ground: the law is one. The principle displays one breach making a transgressor of the whole. The verse displays the offending: in one. The verse displays the guilt: total. The verse displays the law: a unity. The principle that one offence makes one guilty of all displays the seriousness of even "one point" like partiality: since the law is a unity, the will of one Lawgiver, to break it in a single command is to become a transgressor of the whole and guilty before it. The verse displays the law's unity, grounded in the one Lawgiver (v.11).
11:
"For he that said, Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill. Now if thou commit no adultery, yet if thou kill, thou art become a transgressor of the law." The witnesses note that the one Lawgiver makes any breach a transgression of the whole law. On "he that said, Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill," the grammatical voices note the single authority. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of v.10: "he that said (the one Lawgiver, God), Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill" — the same God gave every command; the authority behind each is one and the same.

On "if thou commit no adultery, yet if thou kill," the witnesses note the example. Barnes and Calvin note the illustration: a man may keep one command (no adultery) yet break another (kill); but since the same God forbade both, breaking either defies that one authority.

On "thou art become a transgressor of the law," the grammatical-historical note the conclusion: such a man "art become a transgressor of the law" — by breaking one command he has transgressed the law (as the expression of God's one will); the unity of the Lawgiver makes any single breach a transgression of the whole. The verse displays that the one Lawgiver makes any breach a transgression of the whole.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the single authority — "he that said, Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill," the same God giving every command (Barnes, Robertson); "if thou commit no adultery, yet if thou kill," keeping one yet breaking another still defying the one authority (Barnes, Calvin); "thou art become a transgressor of the law," one breach transgressing the whole law as God's one will (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the one Lawgiver. The verse marks the commands: from one God. The verse displays the example: adultery and killing. The verse displays the breach: of one. The verse displays the result: transgressor of all. The teaching displays the one authority behind every command. The verse displays the Lawgiver: one. The verse displays the killing: a breach. The verse displays the transgressor: of the law. That the one Lawgiver makes any breach a transgression of the whole law displays the ground of the principle: since the same God who forbade adultery also forbade killing, to break either is to defy that one authority and become a transgressor of the law. The verse displays the one Lawgiver, before whose law of liberty all are judged (v.12).
12:
"So speak ye, and so do, as they that shall be judged by the law of liberty." The brief verse notes the call to speak and act as those judged by the law of liberty. The grammatical voices note the standard of judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation: "so speak ye, and so do (let both word and deed be governed), as they that shall be judged by the law of liberty (the gospel law, the perfect law that frees, 1:25)" — live, in word and deed, as those who will be judged by the gospel's law of liberty.

On "speak ye, and so do," the witnesses note word and deed. Barnes and Calvin note that both speech and action are to be regulated by the consciousness of coming judgment; the believer governs his whole conduct, knowing he must answer for it.

On "the law of liberty," the grammatical-historical note the gospel standard: believers will be "judged by the law of liberty" — not the bondage of the old law but the gospel, which frees yet also judges; the law of liberty is the rule by which the Christian's life is measured. The verse displays the call to speak and act as those judged by the law of liberty.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "so speak ye, and so do, as they that shall be judged by the law of liberty (the gospel law that frees, 1:25)," governing word and deed as those to be judged by the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); both speech and action regulated by coming judgment (Barnes, Calvin); "the law of liberty" the gospel standard by which the Christian is measured (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rule of life. The verse marks the speaking: so. The verse displays the doing: so. The verse displays the judgment: by the law of liberty. The verse displays the standard: the gospel. The call displays word and deed governed by coming judgment. The verse displays the speaking: regulated. The verse displays the doing: governed. The verse displays the liberty: the law. The call to speak and act as those judged by the law of liberty displays the practical rule: let both word and deed be governed by the consciousness that you will be judged by the gospel's law of liberty — the standard by which the Christian's whole life is measured. The verse displays the judging law of liberty, which shows no mercy to the merciless (v.13).
13:
"For he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy; and mercy rejoiceth against judgment." The witnesses note that the merciless face judgment without mercy, but mercy triumphs. On "he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn rule: "he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy" — the one who has shown no mercy to others (as in despising the poor) will himself be judged without mercy; God deals with men as they deal with others (cf. Matthew 5:7; 18:35).

On "judgment without mercy," the witnesses note the warning. Barnes and Calvin note that the unmerciful — those who, like the partial, show no compassion to the poor and needy — will find no mercy in their own judgment; mercilessness is a mark of an unrenewed heart and brings a merciless verdict.

On "mercy rejoiceth against judgment," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the triumph of mercy: "mercy rejoiceth against (triumphs over, glories against) judgment" — for the merciful, mercy prevails over judgment; the one who has shown mercy finds that mercy exults over the sentence; God's mercy triumphs, and the merciful are met with mercy. The verse displays that the merciless face judgment without mercy, but mercy triumphs.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rule — "he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy," the merciless judged without mercy, God dealing as men deal (Matthew 5:7, Barnes, Robertson); the unmerciful finding no mercy, mercilessness a mark of an unrenewed heart (Barnes, Calvin); "mercy rejoiceth against (triumphs over) judgment," mercy prevailing for the merciful, God's mercy exulting over the sentence (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rule of mercy. The verse marks the merciless: judged without mercy. The verse displays the principle: as men deal. The verse displays the triumph: mercy over judgment. The verse displays the hope: for the merciful. The teaching displays the merciless condemned, the merciful exalted. The verse displays the judgment: without mercy. The verse displays the mercy: shown. The verse displays the rejoicing: over judgment. That the merciless face judgment without mercy, but mercy triumphs, displays the solemn and hopeful close of the argument: the one who showed no mercy will be judged without mercy — but mercy rejoices against judgment, triumphing for those who have shown it. The verse displays the triumph of mercy, leading to the great question of faith and works (v.14).
14:
"What doth it profit, my brethren, though a man say he hath faith, and have not works? can faith save him?" The witnesses note the question whether a workless faith can save. On "though a man say he hath faith, and have not works," the grammatical voices note the empty profession. Barnes and Robertson note the case set up: "though a man say (claims, professes) he hath faith, and have not works" — the test case is a mere profession of faith without the works that genuine faith produces; the emphasis falls on "say" — a faith that is only claimed.

On "what doth it profit," the witnesses note the uselessness. Barnes and Calvin note the question: "what doth it profit (what good is it, what advantage)" — such a workless, professed faith profits nothing; it cannot benefit the man who has only a faith of words.

On "can faith save him," the grammatical-historical note the precise meaning. Barnes and Robertson note the careful sense: "can faith (that faith — the workless, professed faith just described) save him?" — the question is not whether faith saves, but whether this kind of faith (a barren profession) can save; the expected answer is no. James does not deny that true faith saves, but denies that a faith without works (which is no true faith) can save. The verse displays the question whether a workless faith can save.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the empty profession — "though a man say (professes) he hath faith, and have not works," a mere claimed faith without works (Barnes, Robertson); "what doth it profit," such a faith profiting nothing (Barnes, Calvin); "can faith (that workless faith) save him?" the question not whether faith saves but whether this barren faith can, the answer no, James denying that a faith without works (no true faith) saves, not that true faith saves (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the workless faith. The verse marks the profession: says he hath faith. The verse displays the lack: no works. The verse displays the question: can it profit. The verse displays the precise sense: can that faith save. The question displays the barren faith unable to save. The verse displays the faith: professed. The verse displays the works: absent. The verse displays the saving: questioned. The question whether a workless faith can save displays the heart of the chapter's great argument: if a man merely claims to have faith but has no works, that faith profits nothing — can such a faith save him? The expected answer is no; a faith that produces no works is no saving faith. The verse displays the barren faith, exposed by the empty word to the needy (vv.15-16).
15:
"If a brother or sister be naked, and destitute of daily food," The brief verse notes the case of a brother or sister in need. The grammatical voices note the illustration. Barnes and Robertson note the example introduced to expose empty faith: "if a brother or sister (a fellow-Christian, man or woman) be naked (poorly clad, ill-clothed), and destitute (lacking) of daily food" — the case of a needy fellow-believer, without adequate clothing or food.

On "naked," the witnesses note the want of clothing. Barnes and JFB note that "naked" here means not wholly unclothed but ill-clad, lacking sufficient or proper clothing; the figure of real poverty and need.

On "destitute of daily food," the grammatical-historical note the want of food: "destitute of daily food" — lacking even the day's necessary sustenance; the brother or sister is in genuine, pressing need of the basic necessities of life. The verse displays the case of a brother or sister in need.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the example — "if a brother or sister (fellow-Christian) be naked (ill-clad), and destitute of daily food," a needy fellow-believer without adequate clothing or food (Barnes, Robertson); "naked" meaning ill-clad, lacking proper clothing, real poverty (Barnes, JFB); "destitute of daily food," lacking the day's necessary sustenance, in pressing need (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the needy believer. The verse marks the brother: or sister. The verse displays the want: naked. The verse displays the lack: daily food. The verse displays the need: pressing. The case displays a fellow-believer in real want. The verse displays the naked: ill-clad. The verse displays the destitute: of food. The verse displays the daily: bread lacking. The case of a brother or sister in need displays the illustration that exposes empty faith: a fellow-Christian, man or woman, ill-clad and lacking even the day's food — in genuine, pressing want of life's necessities. The verse displays the needy believer, met only with empty words (v.16). The case is deliberately concrete: not an abstract want but a known brother or sister, met face to face, whose need is plain and present, so that the reader cannot evade the test by pleading ignorance of the want before him.
16:
"And one of you say unto them, Depart in peace, be ye warmed and filled; notwithstanding ye give them not those things which are needful to the body; what doth it profit?" The witnesses note the empty words that give no help. On "one of you say unto them, Depart in peace, be ye warmed and filled," the grammatical voices note the hollow kindness. Barnes and Robertson note the empty response: "one of you say unto them, Depart in peace (a friendly dismissal), be ye warmed (clothed) and filled (fed)" — fine words of good wishes, sending the needy away with a blessing but nothing more; warm words without warm help.

On "ye give them not those things which are needful to the body," the witnesses note the failure to help. Barnes and Calvin note the contradiction: "ye give them not those things which are needful (necessary) to the body" — for all the kind words, they supply nothing of what the body actually needs; the words are belied by the empty hand.

On "what doth it profit," the grammatical-historical note the application: "what doth it profit?" — such empty words profit the needy nothing; and just so, a faith of mere words, without the works of love, profits nothing; the parallel is exact — as words without deeds are useless to the poor, so faith without works is useless for salvation. The verse displays the empty words that give no help.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hollow kindness — "Depart in peace, be ye warmed and filled," fine words sending the needy away with a blessing but nothing more (Barnes, Robertson); "ye give them not those things which are needful to the body," the words belied by the empty hand, supplying nothing (Barnes, Calvin); "what doth it profit?" empty words profiting nothing, just as faith without works profits nothing — the exact parallel (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the empty words. The verse marks the saying: be warmed and filled. The verse displays the failure: no help given. The verse displays the question: what profit. The verse displays the parallel: faith without works. The empty words display kind speech that helps nothing, like workless faith. The verse displays the words: hollow. The verse displays the help: withheld. The verse displays the profit: none. The empty words that give no help display the exact parallel to workless faith: to tell the needy "be warmed and filled" while giving them nothing the body needs profits them nothing — and just so, a faith of mere words, without the works of love, profits nothing. The verse displays the useless words, proving faith without works dead (v.17).
17:
"Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone." The brief verse notes that faith without works is dead. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note the application of the parable: "even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead (nekra — lifeless, devoid of vitality), being alone (by itself, in itself)" — just as kind words without deeds are useless, so faith that produces no works is dead in itself; a lifeless thing.

On "is dead," the witnesses note the lifelessness. Barnes and Calvin note that such faith "is dead" — not merely weak but lifeless, like a corpse; a faith that bears no fruit of works has no spiritual life; it is dead faith, not the living faith that saves.

On "being alone," the grammatical-historical note the isolation: "being alone (by itself)" — faith standing alone, without works, is dead; works are the necessary expression of living faith, and faith that remains by itself, producing nothing, proves itself lifeless. The verse displays that faith without works is dead.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conclusion — "even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead (lifeless), being alone (by itself)," faith producing no works dead in itself, like useless words (Barnes, Robertson); "is dead," not merely weak but lifeless like a corpse, bearing no fruit (Barnes, Calvin); "being alone," faith standing by itself without works proving itself lifeless (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead faith. The verse marks the faith: without works. The verse displays the verdict: dead. The verse displays the state: alone. The verse displays the lifelessness: like a corpse. The conclusion displays workless faith as dead faith. The verse displays the faith: barren. The verse displays the death: spiritual. The verse displays the alone: isolated. That faith without works is dead displays the conclusion of the parable: just as kind words without deeds profit nothing, so faith that has no works is dead in itself — a lifeless thing, by itself producing nothing, not the living faith that saves. The verse displays the dead faith, challenged to show itself without works (v.18).
18:
"Yea, a man may say, Thou hast faith, and I have works: shew me thy faith without thy works, and I will shew thee my faith by my works." The witnesses note the challenge to show faith by works. On "a man may say, Thou hast faith, and I have works," the grammatical voices note the imagined dialogue. Barnes and Robertson note the supposed objector or interlocutor: "a man may say, Thou hast faith, and I have works" — the verse is difficult, but the sense is that one may distinguish the two: you claim faith, I point to works; the challenge tests whether faith can be shown apart from works.

On "shew me thy faith without thy works," the witnesses note the impossible demand. Barnes and Calvin note the challenge: "shew me thy faith without (apart from) thy works" — it is impossible to demonstrate faith that has no works; faith is invisible and can only be evidenced by its fruits; one cannot exhibit a workless faith.

On "I will shew thee my faith by my works," the grammatical-historical note the true demonstration: "I will shew thee my faith by (out of) my works" — works are the visible evidence of faith; the genuine believer proves his faith by his deeds; works make faith manifest. The verse displays the challenge to show faith by works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dialogue — "Thou hast faith, and I have works," distinguishing the two, testing whether faith can be shown apart from works (Barnes, Robertson); "shew me thy faith without thy works," the impossible demand, faith invisible and only evidenced by fruits (Barnes, Calvin); "I will shew thee my faith by my works," works the visible evidence of faith, making it manifest (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the challenge. The verse marks the claim: faith and works. The verse displays the demand: show faith without works. The verse displays the impossibility: faith unseen alone. The verse displays the proof: faith by works. The challenge displays works as the only evidence of faith. The verse displays the faith: claimed. The verse displays the works: shown. The verse displays the demonstration: by deeds. The challenge to show faith by works displays the test of genuine faith: one cannot exhibit a faith that has no works, for faith is invisible — but the true believer shows his faith by his works, which are its visible evidence. The verse displays the demonstrated faith, beyond the bare belief of devils (v.19).
19:
"Thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well: the devils also believe, and tremble." The witnesses note that even the devils believe and tremble. On "thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well," the grammatical voices note the bare creed. Barnes and Robertson note the example of a merely intellectual faith: "thou believest that there is one God (the fundamental creed, the unity of God); thou doest well (so far, good)" — to believe in one God is right and good as far as it goes; orthodox belief is commended.

On "the devils also believe," the witnesses note the demons' faith. Barnes and Calvin note the devastating comparison: "the devils (demons) also believe (this same truth)" — the demons hold the same orthodox belief in one God; a bare assent to truth is something even demons possess; mere creedal faith is no proof of saving faith.

On "and tremble," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the demons' terror: the devils believe "and tremble (phrissousin — shudder, bristle with dread)" — their faith goes beyond cold assent to terror, yet it does not save or sanctify them; it produces dread, not love or obedience; so a faith that is mere belief, even with feeling, without works, is no better than the demons' and cannot save. The verse displays that even the devils believe and tremble.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bare creed — "thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well," orthodox belief commended as far as it goes (Barnes, Robertson); "the devils also believe," the demons holding the same belief, mere assent no proof of saving faith (Barnes, Calvin); "and tremble (shudder with dread)," the demons' faith going beyond assent to terror yet not saving, producing dread not obedience (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the demons' faith. The verse marks the belief: one God. The verse displays the commendation: thou doest well. The verse displays the comparison: the devils believe. The verse displays the terror: they tremble. The example displays bare belief no better than the demons'. The verse displays the believing: orthodox. The verse displays the devils: believing. The verse displays the trembling: with dread. That even the devils believe and tremble displays the devastating exposure of bare faith: to believe in one God is good, but the demons believe the same and shudder with dread — yet their faith neither saves nor sanctifies them; so a faith that is mere belief, without works, is no better than the demons' and cannot save. The verse displays the demons' trembling faith, set against the working faith of Abraham (vv.20-23).
20:
"But wilt thou know, O vain man, that faith without works is dead?" The brief verse notes the appeal to the vain man that faith without works is dead. The grammatical voices note the pointed address. Barnes and Robertson note the challenge: "wilt thou know (are you willing to learn, will you be convinced), O vain (empty, foolish, senseless) man, that faith without works is dead (or barren, useless)?" — James addresses the one who clings to a workless faith as "vain" (empty-headed), and presses the truth that such faith is dead.

On "O vain man," the witnesses note the rebuke. Barnes and Calvin note that "vain man" is a sharp but just rebuke — empty, foolish, deceived; the man who imagines a workless faith can save is senseless; the address prepares for the proof from Abraham and Rahab.

On "faith without works is dead," the grammatical-historical note the thesis restated: "faith without works is dead (some texts: barren, idle)" — the central thesis is repeated and now to be proved by example; faith divorced from works is lifeless and fruitless. The verse displays the appeal to the vain man that faith without works is dead.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the challenge — "wilt thou know (will you be convinced), O vain (empty, foolish) man, that faith without works is dead?" James pressing the truth on the one clinging to workless faith (Barnes, Robertson); "O vain man" a sharp but just rebuke of the senseless, deceived man (Barnes, Calvin); "faith without works is dead (barren)," the thesis restated, to be proved by example (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pointed appeal. The verse marks the address: O vain man. The verse displays the challenge: wilt thou know. The verse displays the thesis: faith without works dead. The verse displays the purpose: to prove it. The appeal displays the senseless man pressed with the truth. The verse displays the vain: rebuked. The verse displays the faith: barren. The verse displays the death: restated. The appeal to the vain man that faith without works is dead displays the pointed transition to the proof: James challenges the empty-headed man who trusts a workless faith to learn that such faith is dead — a thesis now to be demonstrated by Abraham and Rahab. The verse displays the restated thesis, proved by Abraham's offering of Isaac (v.21).
21:
"Was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac his son upon the altar?" The witnesses note Abraham justified by works in offering Isaac — the great faith-and-works crux. On the meaning of "justified by works," interpreters work to reconcile James with Paul. The Reformed Calvin and Gill, with the grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott, hold that James means "justified" in the sense of demonstrated, shown, or proved righteous — not justified before God by the merit of works, but manifested as righteous by works that prove faith genuine: Abraham, justified before God by faith (Genesis 15:6), proved and made manifest the reality of that faith by offering Isaac (Genesis 22), thirty years later. So "justified by works" = shown to be righteous, the works being the evidence, not the ground; there is no contradiction with Paul, who speaks of the initial being-counted-righteous before God by faith. Calvin sharply rejects "the Sophists" (the Roman Catholic reading) who from this verse "cry out as being victorious, that justification is partly by works" — works conferring or meriting righteousness before God's tribunal.

On "when he had offered Isaac his son upon the altar," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke note the act and the harmony. Wesley reconciles by the two justifications and two works: Paul speaks of justification by faith antecedent to works (Genesis 15:6); James of justification "made righteous" by works consequent to faith (the offering of Isaac); James's justification by works is the fruit of Paul's justification by faith. Robertson: James and Paul "use the same words, but they are talking of different acts"; "there is no contradiction at all."

The witnesses agree the reconciliation: James and Paul do not contradict, for they use "justified" in different senses — Paul of the initial counting-righteous before God by faith, James of the demonstration/manifestation of that faith's reality by works (Abraham proved by Isaac what he believed thirty years before). But the readings have a confessional edge: the Protestant consensus (Calvin, Gill, Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Wesley, Clarke) holds works are the evidence, not the ground, of justification; the Roman Catholic / "Sophist" reading (named and rejected by Calvin) takes the verse to teach that man is justified partly by works, works meriting righteousness before God. The verse displays the faith-and-works crux. The verse marks the justification: by works. The verse displays the debate: works as evidence, or works meriting. The verse displays the example: Abraham offering Isaac. The verse displays the harmony: with Paul. The crux displays Abraham's works proving his justifying faith. The verse displays the Abraham: justified. The verse displays the works: shown. The verse displays the Isaac: offered. Abraham justified by works in offering Isaac displays the great faith-and-works crux: "justified by works" read by the Protestant consensus as shown/proved righteous, the works demonstrating a faith that justified before God thirty years earlier (Genesis 15:6) — no contradiction with Paul — and rejected when taken (by the Roman Catholic "Sophists") to mean man is justified partly by the merit of works. The verse displays the demonstrated faith, working with the works (v.22).
22:
"Seest thou how faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect?" The witnesses note that faith wrought with works and was perfected by them. On "seest thou how faith wrought with his works," the grammatical voices note the cooperation. Barnes and Robertson note the relation of faith and works in Abraham: "seest thou how faith wrought with (synergei — co-operated with, worked together with) his works" — Abraham's faith and his works acted together; the faith was the living principle that produced the obedient act of offering Isaac; faith and works cooperated to the result.

On "faith wrought with his works," the witnesses note faith as the active principle. Barnes and Calvin note that faith was not idle but operative — it "wrought with" the works, faith being the root and works the fruit; the faith was the energy behind the deed.

On "by works was faith made perfect," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the Reformed Calvin note the perfecting. Wesley: faith is "made perfect" by works not in its being (faith precedes works) but in its vigour and manifestation — works excite and increase faith. Calvin: faith was "perfected" not because it received its perfection from works, but because it was thereby proved to be true; the works showed the faith complete and genuine. The verse displays that faith wrought with works and was perfected by them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cooperation — "faith wrought with (co-operated with) his works," Abraham's faith and works acting together, faith the living principle producing the deed (Barnes, Robertson); faith the active root, not idle, working with the fruit (Barnes, Calvin); "by works was faith made perfect," faith perfected (proved genuine, its vigour increased) by works, not receiving its being from them (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays faith and works together. The verse marks the cooperation: faith wrought with works. The verse displays the principle: faith active. The verse displays the perfecting: by works. The verse displays the meaning: proved genuine. The relation displays faith working with and perfected by deeds. The verse displays the faith: wrought. The verse displays the works: joined. The verse displays the perfection: attained. That faith wrought with works and was perfected by them displays the relation of the two in Abraham: his faith co-operated with his works, the living faith producing the obedient deed — and by those works his faith was made perfect, proved genuine and brought to its full expression. The verse displays the perfected faith, fulfilling the Scripture that he believed God (v.23).
23:
"And the scripture was fulfilled which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed unto him for righteousness: and he was called the Friend of God." The witnesses note the Scripture fulfilled: Abraham believed and was the Friend of God. On "the scripture was fulfilled which saith, Abraham believed God," the grammatical voices note the confirmation. Barnes and Robertson note the key text (Genesis 15:6): "the scripture was fulfilled (confirmed, carried out to its full expression) which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed (counted, reckoned) unto him for righteousness" — the declaration that Abraham believed and was counted righteous (made long before Isaac's offering) was confirmed and fulfilled by his obedient act; the offering proved the reality of the faith the Scripture records.

On "imputed unto him for righteousness," the Reformed Calvin notes the order. Calvin presses that the imputation of righteousness (Genesis 15:6) preceded the offering of Isaac by some thirty years, so the imputation cannot be grounded on that work; James says the Scripture was "fulfilled" (confirmed) by the later act, not that righteousness was then first imputed; this proves "justified by works" means the manifestation, not the cause, of righteousness.

On "he was called the Friend of God," the grammatical-historical note the honour: Abraham "was called the Friend of God (2 Chronicles 20:7; Isaiah 41:8)" — a title of highest honour, the fruit of his faith and obedience; God took him into intimate friendship. The verse displays the Scripture fulfilled: Abraham believed and was the Friend of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the confirmation — "the scripture was fulfilled (confirmed)... Abraham believed God, and it was imputed... for righteousness" (Genesis 15:6), the declaration of his believing fulfilled by his obedient act (Barnes, Robertson); the imputation preceding the offering by thirty years, so works manifest not cause the righteousness (Calvin); "called the Friend of God," the high honour fruit of faith and obedience (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fulfilled Scripture. The verse marks the believing: Abraham's. The verse displays the imputation: for righteousness. The verse displays the order: faith first. The verse displays the honour: Friend of God. The confirmation displays Abraham's faith proved and honoured. The verse displays the Scripture: fulfilled. The verse displays the imputed: righteousness. The verse displays the Friend: of God. The Scripture fulfilled — Abraham believed and was the Friend of God — displays the proof that "justified by works" means manifestation, not merit: the declaration that Abraham believed and was counted righteous (Genesis 15:6, long before Isaac's offering) was confirmed by his obedient act, which proved that faith genuine — and he was called the Friend of God. The verse displays the fulfilled Scripture, summed up in justification not by faith only (v.24).
24:
"Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only." The witnesses note the conclusion: justified by works, and not by faith only — the crux restated. On "by works a man is justified," interpreters maintain the reconciliation with Paul. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the Reformed Gill, hold "justified" here means shown, declared, or proved righteous, not justified before God by the merit of works: works are the evidence by which a man's faith is shown genuine and he is seen to be a justified man. Barnes: justification "takes place in the sight of God... by faith," but it is "only the faith that produces good works that justifies," so good works, as the expression of faith, are necessary; James "contradicts a false explanation of Paul's doctrine," not Paul. Robertson: "is justified" here is "not 'is made righteous,' but 'is shown to be righteous.'"

On "not by faith only," the witnesses note the careful qualification. Ellicott notes that James says a man is not justified "by faith only" (the adverb emphatic) — not denying justification by faith, but that "fancied" justification by an idle, speculative, theoretic faith with no acts of love. Wesley: Paul and James "do not speak of the same faith" (Paul of living faith, James of dead faith) "nor the same works" (Paul of works antecedent to faith, James of works subsequent); so there is no contradiction.

The witnesses agree the reconciliation: "not by faith only" condemns not faith but a barren, workless faith; the faith that justifies is a faith that works; works are the necessary evidence of justifying faith, not its meritorious ground (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Wesley, Ellicott). The same confessional edge stands as at v.21: the Protestant consensus holds works the evidence, the Roman Catholic reading (rejected) takes the verse to teach justification partly by the merit of works. The verse displays the faith-and-works crux restated. The verse marks the justification: by works. The verse displays the qualification: not by faith only. The verse displays the debate: works as evidence, or works meriting. The verse displays the harmony: with Paul. The conclusion displays the working faith that alone justifies. The verse displays the works: justifying as evidence. The verse displays the faith: not alone. The verse displays the justification: shown. The conclusion — justified by works, and not by faith only — displays the crux restated: "not by faith only" condemns not faith but a dead, workless faith, the Protestant consensus holding that the justifying faith is one that produces works (the evidence, not the ground, of justification), no contradiction with Paul, while the rejected Roman Catholic reading takes it to teach justification partly by the merit of works. The verse displays the working faith, confirmed by the example of Rahab (v.25).
25:
"Likewise also was not Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she had received the messengers, and had sent them out another way?" The witnesses note Rahab also justified by works, proving faith genuine. On "was not Rahab the harlot justified by works," the grammatical voices note the second example. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with Abraham: "likewise also was not Rahab the harlot justified by works" — Rahab, as different from Abraham as possible (a Gentile, a woman, a harlot), is the second proof; she too was "justified by works" in the same sense — shown to be righteous, her faith proved genuine by her act.

On "when she had received the messengers, and had sent them out another way," the witnesses note her act of faith. Barnes and Calvin note the deed (Joshua 2): "when she had received (welcomed, sheltered) the messengers (the Israelite spies), and had sent them out another way (helped them escape)" — Rahab's reception of the spies and aid in their escape was the work that demonstrated her faith in the God of Israel.

On the pairing, the Reformed Calvin notes the design: James joins Abraham, the most eminent patriarch, and Rahab, a harlot and alien, to show that no one of any condition is counted righteous without good works; whosoever would be counted righteous, even the lowest, must show it by works. The verse displays Rahab also justified by works, proving faith genuine.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second example — "was not Rahab the harlot justified by works," Rahab, as different from Abraham as possible, the second proof, shown righteous by her act (Barnes, Robertson); "received the messengers, and sent them out another way" (Joshua 2), her reception and aid of the spies the work demonstrating faith (Barnes, Calvin); the pairing of patriarch and harlot showing none of any condition is justified without works (Calvin). The verse displays the second proof. The verse marks the example: Rahab. The verse displays the work: received the spies. The verse displays the aid: sent them out. The verse displays the demonstration: faith proved. The example displays Rahab's faith shown by her deed. The verse displays the harlot: justified. The verse displays the messengers: received. The verse displays the works: proving faith. Rahab also justified by works, proving faith genuine, displays the second confirmation of the thesis: Rahab the harlot — as unlike Abraham as could be — was also justified by works when she received the spies and helped them escape, her deed proving her faith genuine; so none, however lowly, is shown righteous without works. The verse displays the proved faith, as body and spirit prove the living whole (v.26).
26:
"For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also." The witnesses note the closing figure: faith without works is dead as a body without the spirit. On "as the body without the spirit is dead," the grammatical voices note the comparison. Barnes and Robertson note the apt figure: "as the body without the spirit (pneuma — breath, the animating principle, the life) is dead (a lifeless corpse)" — the body, when the spirit (life-breath) has departed, is a dead carcass; the comparison draws on the most obvious mark of death.

On "so faith without works is dead also," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note the point: "so faith without works is dead also" — faith without works is as lifeless as a corpse; works are to faith what the breath/life is to the body — the evidence and accompaniment of life. Calvin cautions the figure does not make works the life of faith (that would reverse the truth), but means that faith without works is a dead carcass, lifeless.

On the figure's force, the grammatical-historical note the conclusion of the argument: as a body without the animating spirit is plainly dead, so a faith that produces no works is plainly dead — the whole argument summed up; living faith works, dead faith does not. The verse displays the closing figure: faith without works is dead as a body without the spirit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comparison — "as the body without the spirit (life-breath) is dead (a corpse)," the body without its animating principle a lifeless carcass (Barnes, Robertson); "so faith without works is dead also," faith without works lifeless as a corpse, works the evidence of life (Barnes, Calvin); Calvin cautioning the figure does not make works the life of faith but likens workless faith to a dead body (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the closing figure. The verse marks the body: without the spirit. The verse displays the death: a corpse. The verse displays the faith: without works. The verse displays the verdict: dead also. The figure displays workless faith dead as a lifeless body. The verse displays the body: lifeless. The verse displays the spirit: departed. The verse displays the faith: dead. The closing figure — faith without works is dead as a body without the spirit — displays the summing-up of the whole argument: as a body from which the animating spirit has departed is a lifeless corpse, so a faith that produces no works is dead; living faith works, dead faith does not. The verse displays the dead faith, closing the great chapter on partiality and the faith that works.
